summary: Gruber argues that the handwringing over Apple dropping 'Computer' from its corporate name is misguided. Rather than abandoning computers for consumer electronics, Apple's advantage is that the entire consumer electronics industry is becoming computers — and Apple makes better computers, OS, and software than anyone. He uses Apple TV as proof: under the hood it's a Mac running a modified Mac OS X, offering both a simple mass-market interface and deep hackability for nerds. The device exemplifies Apple's enduring strategy of picking important features, implementing them well, and presenting them simply.
key_points: The consumer electronics industry is shifting toward making computers, which plays to Apple's core strengths in hardware, OS, and software design
Apple TV proves the point: it runs a modified Mac OS X under the hood, making it fundamentally a computer disguised as a consumer device
Apple TV strikes a balance between simplicity for regular users and deep hackability for nerds, contradicting the 'too closed' criticism
Apple is arguably more of a computer company now than 10 years ago, when pundits called for them to exit hardware entirely
The iPod strategy — pick important features, implement them well, present them obviously — extends naturally to Apple TV
money_quotes: The entire consumer electronics industry is shifting toward making computers; Apple's advantage is that they design better computers, have a better OS, and write better software than any of their competitors.
They're arguably more of a computer company now than they were 10 years ago.
For most of the 1990s, there were dozens of jackass columnists and 'industry analysts' calling for Apple to do just that.
Great software, well-integrated with cleverly-designed hardware — which pretty much describes every successful product Apple has shipped in the last 30 years.
Pick the important features, implement them really well, and present them in an obvious UI.
Apple's stance on Apple TV hacking is clearly neutral, not antagonistic.
Regular people never have to get their hands dirty with complex nerdery, but those of us who want to easily can.
key_insight: Apple dropping 'Computer' from its name isn't a retreat from computers — it's recognition that all consumer electronics are becoming computers, which is exactly where Apple's strengths lie.
